replies.text: Isn't that basically what Wicked was even in the 1990s? Or what the prior film about the bride from 1985 was? The original 'bride' in the book was destroyed because Frankenstein didn't want a potential race of monsters. In the 1935 film, the monster destroys himself, the bride, and Pretorius because he realized the two creatures were abominations and Pretorius was too as an evil human. The 1985 film then reframed the monster and bride into a love story, and women also have projected a more feminist take onto the 1935 movie retroactively that the monster killing the bride was 'incel' behavior or misogynistic. So this kind of fanfic retroactive interpretations isn't new. I just love the unintentional irony of women wanting to be monsters and attracted to monsters. The original messaging was that all these creatures are bad, as is trying to play god. Presumably because the author was a woman, modern women have now latched onto it with the lens of their modern politics.

replies.author: @terencejay8845
replies.text: I'm reading a book from the late 90's about the Hollywood 'spec script' business. Mostly the stories of how spec scripts are passed around, some inviting crazy bidding wars, some languishing for years with just one person pushing them around. What is common is a studio paying $1.5 Mill for a brilliant novel spec script, then it gets 'developed', packaged and butchered by Executives and 'Stars' until the writer walks off wanting nothing to do with it. I'm sure all scripts start with the greatest intentions, but what ends up on the screen is a dog's dinner that makes the original writer weep.

author: @matthewsmith4483
text: Mary Shelley wasnt even that big of a feminist for her time. She advocated for a more slow-paced reform where women had more rights, but still mostly focused on the family. Mary Shelley was a woman who had many miscarriages, and when she finally did have a kid that survived, her husband died in a storm. So her focus was always about her family and her struggles with having a child. Look at Frankenstein about life, parenthood, and the anxiety she feels from it. Her ideas of feminism would actually be very conservative by today's standards. Disrespecting the legacy of a woman who had such a tough life is a really bad look. She eventually died of brain cancer.
